title: An Ancient Philosophical Song Reconstructed and Played for the First Time in 1,000 Years
description:
in History, Music |   	April 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Above and below, you can watchВ musiВӯcians perВӯform вҖңSongs of ConВӯsoВӯlaВӯtion,вҖқ a 1
body:
in History, Music |   	April 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Above and below, you can watchВ musiВӯcians perВӯform вҖңSongs of ConВӯsoВӯlaВӯtion,вҖқ a 1,000-year-old song set вҖңto the poetВӯic porВӯtions of Roman philosoВӯpher BoethiusвҖҷ magВӯnum opus The ConВӯsoВӯlaВӯtion of PhiВӯlosВӯoВӯphy,вҖқ an influВӯenВӯtial medieval text writВӯten durВӯing the 6th cenВӯtuВӯry. AccordВӯing to CamВӯbridge UniВӯverВӯsiВӯty, the perВӯforВӯmance of the piece, which had been lost in time until recentВӯly,В didВӯnвҖҷt come easВӯiВӯly:
[T]he task of perВӯformВӯing such ancient works today is not as simВӯple as readВӯing and playВӯing the music in front of you. 1,000 years ago, music was writВӯten in a way that recordВӯed melodВӯic outВӯlines, but not вҖҳnotesвҖҷ as todayвҖҷs musiВӯcians would recogВӯnise them; relyВӯing on aurВӯal traВӯdiВӯtions and the memВӯoВӯry of musiВӯcians to keep them alive. Because these aurВӯal traВӯdiВӯtions died out in the 12th cenВӯtuВӯry, it has often been thought imposВӯsiВӯble to reconВӯstruct вҖҳlostвҖҷ music from this era вҖ“ preВӯciseВӯly because the pitchВӯes are unknown.
Now, after more than two decades of painstakВӯing work on idenВӯtiВӯfyВӯing the techВӯniques used to set parВӯticВӯuВӯlar verse forms, research underВӯtakВӯen by CamВӯbridge UniversityвҖҷs Dr Sam BarВӯrett has enabled him to reconВӯstruct melodies from the redisВӯcovВӯered leaf of theВ 11thВ cenВӯtuВӯry вҖҳCamВӯbridge SongsвҖҷ.
The song is perВӯformed hereВ byВ BenВӯjamin BagВӯby, HanВӯna MarВӯti and NorВӯbert Rodenkirchen, three memВӯbers of the medieval music ensemВӯble known asВ SequenВӯtia.


	

Note: An earВӯliВӯer verВӯsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
If you would like to supВӯport the misВӯsion of Open CulВӯture, conВӯsidВӯer makВӯingВ a donaВӯtion to our site. ItвҖҷs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conВӯtriВӯbuВӯtions will help us conВӯtinВӯue proВӯvidВӯing the best free culВӯturВӯal and eduВӯcaВӯtionВӯal mateВӯriВӯals to learnВӯers everyВӯwhere. You can conВӯtribute through PayВӯPal, PatreВӯon, and VenВӯmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:
What Ancient Greek Music SoundВӯed Like: LisВӯten to a ReconВӯstrucВӯtion ThatвҖҷs вҖң100% AccuВӯrateвҖқ
Hear the OldВӯest Song in the World: A SumerВӯian Hymn WritВӯten 3,400 Years Ago
See The GuidonВӯian Hand, the Medieval SysВӯtem for ReadВӯing Music, Get Brought Back to Life
Hear the Song WritВӯten on a SinnerвҖҷs ButВӯtock in HieronyВӯmus BoschвҖҷs PaintВӯingВ The GarВӯden of EarthВӯly Delights

title: What Happens When a Globalized World Collapses: Archaeologist Eric Cline Explains How Bronze Age Civilizations  Adapted, Survived or Vanished
description:
in History |   	April 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We live, as weвҖҷre often told, in the era of globВӯalВӯizaВӯtion. In fact, weвҖҷve been told i
body:
in History |   	April 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We live, as weвҖҷre often told, in the era of globВӯalВӯizaВӯtion. In fact, weвҖҷve been told it so often over the past few decades that it now hardВӯly seems like an obserВӯvaВӯtion worth makВӯing. But howВӯevВӯer thorВӯoughВӯly our era is defined by conВӯnecВӯtions between far-flung nations, sociВӯeties, economies, and culВӯtures, we shouldВӯnвҖҷt flatВӯter ourВӯselves into thinkВӯing we are pioВӯneers in a wholВӯly new globВӯalВӯized realВӯiВӯty. As clasВӯsiВӯcist Eric Cline explains in this recent Big Think interВӯview,В an interВӯconВӯnectВӯed world flourВӯished in the late Bronze Age, and espeВӯcialВӯly the fourВӯteenth and thirВӯteenth cenВӯturies BC. вҖңLife was pretВӯty goodвҖқ in those days, he says, at least if you lived in one of the lands around the MediterВӯranean and Near East that conВӯstiВӯtutВӯed what he calls the вҖңancient G8.вҖқ
The memВӯber peoВӯples of this retВӯroВӯspecВӯtive orgaВӯniВӯzaВӯtion includВӯed the MyceВӯnaeans and Minoans in Greece, the HitВӯtites in modВӯern-day Turkey, the AssyrВӯiВӯans and the BabyВӯloВӯniВӯans in modВӯern-day Iraq, as well as the CypriВӯots, EgypВӯtians, and CanaanВӯites. Alas, as implied by the title of ClineвҖҷs 1177 BC: The Year CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtion ColВӯlapsed, their good times togethВӯer didВӯnвҖҷt last.
In that book, and in lecВӯtures on YouTube, heвҖҷs explained the variВӯety of facВӯtors that conВӯtributed to the disВӯsoВӯluВӯtion of that once prosВӯperВӯous вҖңsmall-world netВӯwork.вҖқ His surВӯprisВӯing popВӯuВӯlarВӯiВӯty for a hisВӯtoВӯriВӯan of the Bronze Age owes in part to his willВӯingВӯness to draw comВӯparВӯisons with that time and our own.В Many of his fans sureВӯly found him out of curiosВӯiВӯty over one quesВӯtion: is our вҖңflatвҖқ twenВӯty-first-cenВӯtuВӯry world simВӯiВӯlarВӯly headВӯed for a colВӯlapse?


	

If so, we might pay less attenВӯtion to why the ancient G8 colВӯlapsed, and more to what became of its forВӯmerВӯly interВӯdeВӯpenВӯdent sociВӯeties when the criВӯsis had run its course. Such is the subВӯject of ClineвҖҷs After 1177 B.C.: The SurВӯvival of CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtions, and of the Big Think interВӯview extract at the top of the post. Some coped, some adaptВӯed, some transВӯformed, and othВӯers simВӯply vanВӯished. CypriВӯots and the PhoeniВӯcians of Canaan, for examВӯple, remade themВӯselves to thrive in the chaos; the EgypВӯtians mudВӯdled through with a mixВӯture of adapВӯtaВӯtion and copВӯing; the MyceВӯnaeans and the Minoans lost more or less everyВӯthing, includВӯing their writВӯing sysВӯtem, and had to rebuild from square one. But the truВӯly cauВӯtionВӯary tale is that of the HitВӯtites, whose civВӯiВӯlizaВӯtionВӯal anniВӯhiВӯlaВӯtion appears to have been in large part self-inflictВӯed.В вҖңDonвҖҷt be a HitВӯtite,вҖқ is one of ClineвҖҷs pieces of advice; anothВӯer is to gain an underВӯstandВӯing of antifragiliВӯtyВ soonВӯer rather than latВӯer.
RelatВӯed conВӯtent:
Why CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtion ColВӯlapsed in 1177 BC: Watch ClasВӯsiВӯcist Eric ClineвҖҷs LecВӯture That Has Already GarВӯnered 7.6 MilВӯlion Views
Is AmerВӯiВӯca DeclinВӯing Like Ancient Rome?
GГ¶bekВӯli Tepe: The 12,000-Year-Old Ruins That Rewrite the StoВӯry of CivВӯiВӯlizaВӯtion
Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

title: Navid Baraty's Atmospheric Photos Explore Contrasting Scales of Time
description: When we consider that enormous metropolises like New York City and Chicago have only come into being within the past few hundred years, itвҖҷs impos
body:
When we consider that enormous metropolises like New York City and Chicago have only come into being within the past few hundred years, itвҖҷs impossible not to stand in awe of ancient cultural sites that have existed for millennia or geological features that expose millionsвҖ”even billionsвҖ”of years of the planetвҖҷs natural history. For Navid Baraty, the contrasts and tensions of contemporary urban life and timeless landscapes merge in otherworldly photographs.
BaratyвҖҷs series The Time Between juxtaposes cityscapes with dramatic terrain, from desert dunes to snow-capped mountains. The project revolves around images in which two distinct digital photographs converge in a composite, drawing on the film technique of double exposure and exploring ideas of permanence, presence, and the вҖңspace between different scales of time,вҖқ the artist says.

The Time Between highlights iconic skylines like Manhattan and Chicago, pairing the outlines of skyscrapers and city lights with dramatic rock formations and atmospheric effects. вҖңThe process is intentional but restrained, combining the two frames through careful blending rather than constructing or artificially generating new elements,вҖқ Baraty tells Colossal. вҖңIвҖҷm interested in combinations that feel impossible at first, yet strangely convincing once they come together.вҖқ
See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Navid Baraty's Atmospheric Photos Explore Contrasting Scales of Time

title: An Interactive Archive Celebrates the Wide Ranging Projects Inviting 'Unruly Play'
description: вҖңPlay is how we give permission,вҖқ says Vitor Freire, co-founder of the Amsterdam-based studio Imagination of Things. вҖңPermission to challenge what
body:
вҖңPlay is how we give permission,вҖқ says Vitor Freire, co-founder of the Amsterdam-based studio Imagination of Things. вҖңPermission to challenge whatвҖҷs fixed, rehearse what doesnвҖҷt exist yet, and close the distance between people who wouldnвҖҷt otherwise meet.вҖқ
Freire and co-founder Monique Grimord take play seriously and, in a new project, their studio created a vast repository of 169 artworks, designs, games, and more that have offered an unexpected encounter with imagination and joy. From Rael San FratelloвҖҷs award-winning вҖңTeeter-Totter WallвҖқ to the healing Wind Phone project to a 12-foot puppet walking the world, Unruly Play is a multi-decade archive of participatory projects, public spaces, and digital creations that invite surprise and camaraderie.
вҖңOur collaborators have always asked us where our ideas come from,вҖқ Gimrod says, вҖңand the truth is that they come from references that rarely talk to each otherвҖ”it can be a seesaw through a border wall or a phone booth connected to the deadвҖҰ We wanted to create unusual dialogues and support new creative practices, and Unruly Play was our answer for that.вҖқ
Fully interactive, the project is searchable by theme or browsable through a shuffle feature. To dive deeper into the power of play, check out this compendium of artist-designed spaces.

schema.headline: An Interactive Archive Celebrates the Wide Ranging Projects Inviting 'Unruly Play'

title: Contrast Reigns in Austn Fischer's Conspicuous Black-and-White Photos
description: вҖңI started doing photographyВ as a way to express things I donвҖҷt understand or to convey a message IвҖҷm having a hard time explaining,вҖқ Austn Fische
body:
вҖңI started doing photographyВ as a way to express things I donвҖҷt understand or to convey a message IвҖҷm having a hard time explaining,вҖқ Austn Fischer says. вҖңI often work in quite a backwardsВ way, knowing exactly what I want to arrange in front of the camera but struggling to understand the significanceВ in my life until I am able to reflect on it after.вҖқ
The Wisconsin-born, London-based photographer taps into fashion as performance, considering how our garments, style, and gestures convey parts of our identities. Contrast is key in FischerвҖҷs work, and it emerges through unusual pairings like lace ruffs atop athletic garb or an angular, black gown with a dainty, horse-shaped wire armature. Whether a portrait or a more conceptual composition, each work harnesses an exuberant sense of play and homes in on our ability to remake ourselves anew.
David Byrne
вҖңGrowing up, I had a lot of questions around my sexuality and my own experience as a man. I naturally gravitated towards fashion because of the story clothing can convey in an image,вҖқ he adds. вҖңThe colours, shape, fabric, and the way clothing wraps around a model create a unique conversation around identity and the body.вҖқ
Fischer has collaborated with a range of editorial and commercial clients, shooting Ai Weiwei with a milkshake for The New Statesman, for example, and David Byrne seemingly under oath for Crack Magazine. Whether working on a personal project or a commissioned series, the photographer transforms a largely black-and-white palette that could appear harsh into scenes exuding warmth and softness. 
Better understanding his own emotions is FischerвҖҷs priority at the moment, following a series of painful experiences, both personal and professional, that have influenced what and how heвҖҷs creating. вҖңRecently, I spent two weeks from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.В paintingВ a wall in a church white over and over to understandВ patience and reflect on myself. IвҖҷm working a lot on understanding emotion and myself through putting my body through different tasks or challenges,вҖқ he tells Colossal.
Explore more of FischerвҖҷs portfolio on his website and Instagram.

Ai Weiwei

David Byrne

schema.headline: Contrast Reigns in Austn Fischer's Conspicuous Black-and-White Photos

title: Water Samples from Around the World Melt into Dima Rebus' Dreamy Paintings
description: The act of painting is often seen as a solitary pursuit; we picture the artist alone in a studio, working through compositional puzzles and experi
body:
The act of painting is often seen as a solitary pursuit; we picture the artist alone in a studio, working through compositional puzzles and experimenting with materials of their own choosing. For Dima Rebus, the process is collaborative, although she may or may not know the other participants. In her large-scale works, the London-based artist adds new meaning to вҖңwatercolorвҖқ as she incorporates water samples collected from strangers around the globe.
In her series Floaters, Rebus processes these crowdsourced units by freezing them with watercolor pigments, which she then allows to melt across the substrate, creating abstract color fields. She then adds figures and elements of landscape, often with a fluid, rippling effect evocative of light glinting off the surface of a lake, as aquatic themes emerge in the form of pools and swimmers.
вҖңNoon FloatersвҖқ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 140 x 300 centimeters
вҖңNearly every sample arrives with a letter, opening a dialogue shaped by place, mood, memory, and time,вҖқ Rebus says. вҖңOver the years, IвҖҷve built an archive of waters from rain, rivers, seas, oceans, and glaciers, each preserved as both material record and human message.вҖқ
Find more on the artistвҖҷs Instagram.
Detail of вҖңNoon FloatersвҖқ
вҖңAfterimage VIIвҖқ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, and rainwater, 110 x 200 centimeters
вҖңNothing Matters Until An Empty Sofa Says Otherwise 1вҖқ (2026), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 110 x 86 centimeters
вҖңIntuitive Course VIIвҖқ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 42 x 80 centimeters
вҖңMorning FloatersвҖқ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 140 x 300 centimeters

schema.headline: Water Samples from Around the World Melt into Dima Rebus' Dreamy Paintings

title: Thousands of Strips of Silk Undulate in Kenny Nguyen's 'Deconstructed Paintings'
description: Silk has been crafted in Vietnam for centuries, where itвҖҷs treasured as a lightweight, luxurious fabric used in traditional garments and art. For
body:
Silk has been crafted in Vietnam for centuries, where itвҖҷs treasured as a lightweight, luxurious fabric used in traditional garments and art. For Kenny Nguyen, who was born in Ben Tre Province and is currently based in Charlotte, North Carolina, the material provides the foundation for vibrant, large-scale wall works that combine elements of weaving and tapestries, garment production, painting, and sculpture.
Using thousands of hand-cut strips of silk, Nguyen draws on his background in fashion design, employing techniques such as pinning, weaving, sewing, and layering to create what he describes as вҖңdeconstructed paintings.вҖқ Each work is created around a kind of imaginary body, its creases and undulating forms evoking movement and versatility.
вҖңUndercurrent Series No. 10вҖқ (2026), hand-cut silk fabric, acrylic paint, and canvas, mounted on wall, 64 x 158 x 8 inches
Even though the works appear fixed, they are actually very malleable. Every time a piece is installed, its undulations are determined by where the pins are placed, and it assumes something of a new version. This complements NguyenвҖҷs approach to silk as a kind of вҖңsecond skin,вҖқ he says, which adapts to its environment, represents memories and heritage, and serves as a metaphor for his personal identity.
NguyenвҖҷs work is currently on view in Textile Art Redefined at Saatchi Gallery in London and Earth to Sky at Sundaram Tagore Gallery in Singapore. His work will also be included in the exhibition Iris van Herpen: Sculpting the Senses at the Brooklyn Museum, which opens on May 16, and a forthcoming group exhibition at Sundaram TagoreвҖҷs London location. See more on the artistвҖҷs Instagram.
Detail of вҖңUndercurrent Series No. 10вҖқ
вҖңEruption Series No. 86вҖқ (2025), hand-cut silk fabric, acrylic paint, and canvas, mounted on wall, 69 x 53 x 8 inches
Detail of вҖңEruption Series No. 86вҖқ
вҖңEncounter Series No. 62вҖқ (2025), hand-cut silk fabric, acrylic paint, and canvas, mounted on wall, 62 x 115 x 4 inches
Detail of вҖңEncounter Series No. 62вҖқ
вҖңEruption Series No. 45вҖқ (2024), hand-cut silk fabric, acrylic paint, and canvas, mounted on wall, 83 x 113 inches (flat dimensions)
вҖңUndercurrent Series No. 5вҖқ (2026), hand-cut silk fabric, acrylic paint, and canvas, mounted on wall, 67 x 112 x 7 inches
Detail of вҖңUndercurrent Series No. 5вҖқ
Alternate view of вҖңEruption Series No. 78вҖқ
Alternate view of вҖңUndercurrent Series No. 10вҖқ
